mechanism: Participants rate moral judgments across multiple scenarios, with stimuli varied by cultural distance of the evaluated individuals. Responses are aggregated and compared across conditions (own culture vs. different cultures). Analysis identifies whether individuals' moral objectivity judgments decrease as cultural distance increases, testing for systematic variation in response patterns rather than fixed individual trait endorsement.

The analysis examines whether empirical evidence supports the claim that naive judgments about knowledge attributions are context-sensitive. Participants respond to knowledge ascription scenarios in different conversational contexts. Results are compared across conditions to determine if the proposed intuitive pattern (context-dependence) emerges, testing a philosophical hypothesis against behavioral data.

The study collects participant judgments about causality, specifically about causal attributions involving absences or omissions. Responses are tabulated across different conditions and analyzed to characterize how laypeople assign causal responsibility when a cause-of-interest is the absence of a typical agent or action.

Participants complete a cognitive reflection test or read moral dilemmas under time-pressure or time-plenty conditions, with argument strength varied (strong vs. weak). Moral judgment responses are recorded. Analysis examines whether cognitive engagement (measured via reflection task performance or provided deliberation time) predicts utilitarian vs. deontological response patterns, and whether argument persuasiveness depends on deliberation opportunity.

Longitudinal cohort data from over 7,000 adolescents is used to estimate associations between measured fertility expectations at baseline and categorical outcomes (four-year college enrollment, two-year college enrollment). Subgroup analyses examine heterogeneous effects by gender. Logistic regression or correlation estimates are computed to characterize the strength of association, and causal claims about dropout are evaluated using conditional probability comparisons.

Event-history analysis models the transition to sexual inactivity over time for individuals in an ongoing relationship, with covariates including age, health status, marital characteristics, and gender. Logistic regression estimates the odds of sexual inactivity conditional on covariates. Survival-like curves or cumulative incidence estimates characterize the time-dependent probability of transition.

Participants read scenarios describing deterministic or non-deterministic situations and judge whether an agent has free will and moral responsibility. Responses are tabulated and compared to prior experimental findings. Analysis tests whether apparent incompatibilist or compatibilist responses depend on whether determinism is correctly understood by participants to mean absence of bypassing (but causal determination still present).

Bilingual learners complete a retrieval-inhibition task measuring executive suppression ability and phonological judgment tasks (speeded ABX categorization and delayed repetition). Correlation or linear regression estimates the strength of association between inhibition scores and accuracy on perception and production tasks, controlling for vocabulary size (proficiency proxy).

Participants judge whether an agent possesses knowledge about a proposition across multiple scenarios where stakes are varied (high-stakes subject vs. low-stakes subject) while evidence and belief are held equivalent. Judgment responses are compared across stake levels to test whether the likelihood of knowledge ascription decreases as practical consequences become more severe.

Data on organ donation enrollment is collected across jurisdictions and time periods with variation in default policy (opt-in vs. opt-out). Donation rates are compared across default conditions, controlling for other policy factors. Regression or comparison of proportions estimates the magnitude of the default effect on population-level donation behavior.

Participants are induced to behave unethically or think about moral transgressions. Subsequent outcomes are measured: word-completion tasks assess salience of cleansing concepts, product preference questionnaires measure desire for cleaning agents, and observed hand-washing behavior is recorded. Analysis correlates moral threat or transgression exposure with increases in cleansing-concept accessibility and physical cleansing behavior.

A laboratory commons game with spatial resource depletion and temporal dynamics is implemented as a simulation. Multiple participant groups make resource-harvesting decisions over time. Outcome measures include total harvest, resource depletion, and group efficiency. Experimental conditions vary the availability of communication and punishment mechanisms. Analysis compares aggregate outcomes across conditions to assess the causal effect of costly punishment and communication on resource governance.

Participants make a choice between two options and subsequently rate the attractiveness of both the chosen and rejected alternatives before and after a hand-washing intervention. Attraction ratings are compared across cleansing vs. control conditions to test whether physical cleansing eliminates the postdecisional dissonance effect (reduction in chosen-alternative valuation gain).

Participants manipulate objects with varying tactile properties (weight, texture, hardness) or hold objects while making social judgments about unrelated stimuli (people, interactions, negotiations). Judgment responses are recorded and compared across haptic conditions. Analysis estimates the strength of association between tactile stimulus properties and social-judgment outcomes.

In a visual detection task with an irrelevant agent present, reaction times (adults) or looking times (7-month-olds) are recorded as an object's location changes and the agent's beliefs about the location are manipulated. Analysis estimates whether observed reaction/looking-time modulation is larger when the agent's belief state is false (violating agent knowledge) vs. true, controlling for stimulus properties. Statistical tests compare the magnitude of belief-induced modulation.
domain: cognitive psychology, theory of mind, infant cognition

mechanism: Learners study foreign-language word pairs under two regimens: repeated retrieval practice (test-restudy cycles) vs. repeated restudy only. At each cycle, the mediator (bridging concept) used to link cue to target is recorded or inferred. Two components of mediator effectiveness are measured: retrievability (whether the mediator is accessible) and target-elicitation (whether the mediator retrieves the correct target). Final retention test performance is compared across study regimens, and mediator effectiveness components are correlated with learning gain.

Participants imagine consuming a food item repeatedly (varied number of imagined consumptions) or perform control tasks. Subsequent actual consumption amount and desire ratings are measured. Analysis compares food intake and appetite between high-imagined-consumption and low-imagined-consumption conditions, testing whether mental simulation induces habituation (reduced responsiveness and consumption).

Students study scientific text under two conditions: elaborative studying with concept mapping vs. retrieval practice (testing). Learning is measured via cued-recall tests assessing both comprehension and inference-requiring questions, and via concept-map construction. Effect size (difference in performance between retrieval-practice and elaborative-study conditions) is estimated across text topics and test formats.

Students are randomized to write about worries before or not before taking a high-stakes exam. Exam scores are recorded. Analysis compares mean exam scores between intervention and control groups, with subgroup estimates by trait anxiety (habitually anxious vs. non-anxious). Effect size and heterogeneity by anxiety trait are reported.

Participants are posed difficult factual questions and cognitive priming measures are applied (e.g., cues evoking internet/computer). Participants then judge whether they can answer the question from memory or will need to look it up. Recall is tested (memory for the fact itself vs. memory for where to find it). Analysis compares recall performance and memory content (fact vs. location) as a function of expectation-of-access and priming condition.
